relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Laban’s sons saw Jacob’s increase as theft from their father’s house.
Jacob saw Laban’s face had changed toward him.
The Eternal called Jacob home and promised presence on the return.
Jacob called Rachel and Leah into the field, away from the house of Laban.
He named the changed face of Laban and the steady presence of the Divine.
He reminded them of his full-strength service.
Laban had changed wages again and again, but harm had been restrained.
Each wage change turned into increase for Jacob.
The Divine had shifted the livestock away from exploitation and toward Jacob.
Jacob remembered the dream that revealed what was happening in the flocks.
The messenger called him by name, and he answered: Here I am.
The Divine saw all that Laban was doing.
The God of Bethel called Jacob back to the vow-place and to his kindred land.
Rachel and Leah asked whether any inheritance remained for them in their father’s house.
They named their father’s treatment as selling and consuming what should have supported them.
They agreed: what the Divine had taken from Laban belonged to them and their children.
Jacob lifted his family onto camels.
He drove out everything gathered in Paddan-Aram toward Isaac in Canaan.
While Laban was away, Rachel took the household gods.
Jacob left without telling Laban, stealing the heart of the one who had deceived him.
He crossed the River and set his face toward Gilead.
Laban learned of the flight on the third day.
He pursued Jacob and overtook him in Gilead.
In a night dream, the Divine warned Laban not to threaten or manipulate Jacob.
Laban reached Jacob in the hill country.
He accused Jacob of stealing his heart and carrying away his daughters.
He claimed he would have sent them with songs, though his history told another story.
He mourned the farewell he had not received.
He admitted he had power to harm, but the Divine warning restrained him.
Then he asked why his gods had been stolen.
Jacob confessed fear that Laban would take his daughters by force.
Not knowing Rachel’s act, Jacob spoke a dangerous oath over whoever had the gods.
Laban searched the tents and came finally to Rachel’s tent.
Rachel had hidden the household gods beneath her saddle.
She used the way of women to avoid rising, and Laban did not find them.
Jacob’s anger rose, and he demanded the charge against him.
He challenged Laban to produce evidence before the brothers.
He named twenty years of faithful labor.
He had borne losses himself, by day and by night.
Heat, cold, and sleeplessness marked his years of service.
Fourteen years for daughters, six for flock, and ten wage changes: Jacob laid out the cost.
Only the Divine of his fathers had kept him from being sent away empty.
Laban still claimed daughters, children, flocks, and all he could see.
So they turned conflict into covenant witness.
Jacob raised a stone as a pillar.
A heap was gathered, and they ate by it.
Laban and Jacob named the witness heap in their own tongues.
The heap stood as witness between them.
Mizpah became a watchtower boundary between mistrustful men.
The Divine was called as witness over Laban’s daughters.
Heap and pillar marked the boundary.
Neither side was to cross the witness markers for harm.
Ancestral divine names stood over the oath, and Jacob swore by the Fear of Isaac.
Sacrifice and bread sealed the boundary meal.
Laban kissed and blessed his family, then returned to his place.
